diagrams.title: DFD Level 2 - User Management STRIDE diagram

data.name: Admin
data.description: System administrator responsible for user lifecycle and permissions.

threats.title: SQL Injection via User Management Inputs
threats.description: An attacker sends malicious SQL commands through the "Name" or "Email" fields. Since these processes talk to the DB, they can be used to bypass security and modify database records.
threats.mitigation: Use Parameterized Queries (Prepared Statements) and strict server-side input validation/sanitization.

threats.title: SQL Injection via User Management Inputs
threats.description: An attacker sends malicious SQL commands through the "Name" or "Email" fields. Since these processes talk to the DB, they can be used to bypass security and modify database records.
threats.mitigation: Use Parameterized Queries (Prepared Statements) and strict server-side input validation/sanitization.

threats.title: Brute Force and Denial of Service
threats.description: An attacker floods the authentication endpoint with automated login attempts, causing resource exhaustion or locking out legitimate administrators.
threats.mitigation: Implement Rate Limiting, CAPTCHA, and progressive account lockout policies.

threats.title: Administrative Privilege Escalation
threats.description: A user with restricted access exploits a logic flaw in the Role Manager to grant themselves or others "Super Admin" permissions.
threats.mitigation: Implement strict Role-Based Access Control (RBAC) and "Least Privilege" principles.

threats.title: Unauthorized Access to Stored Credentials
threats.description: If the database is compromised, an attacker can read all user profile data and hashed passwords.
threats.mitigation: Encrypt data at rest and use strong hashing algorithms like Argon2 or BCrypt for passwords.

threats.title: Lack of Modification Integrity
threats.description: Changes are made directly to the database without being logged, making it impossible to prove which administrator performed a specific change.
threats.mitigation: Enable database-level auditing and centralized application logs.

threats.title: Injection of Rogue Accounts
threats.description: An attacker modifies the "New User" data packet in transit to create a user with a different email or pre-set password controlled by the attacker.
threats.mitigation: Implement request signing and server-side validation of all input fields.

threats.title: Manipulation of Privilege Commands
threats.description: An attacker intercepts the role assignment request and modifies the "Role ID" to grant a higher privilege before it reaches the server.
threats.mitigation: Use a secure administrative tunnel (VPN) or mutual TLS (mTLS) for authorization commands.

threats.title: Interception of Admin Credentials
threats.description: An attacker intercepts the login credentials (username/password) while they are in transit from the Admin's browser to the Authentication service.
threats.mitigation: Enforce TLS 1.3 encryption and use secure, HTTP-only session cookies.

threats.title: Unauthorized Profile Data Manipulation
threats.description: An attacker intercepts the update request to modify sensitive fields (such as the recovery email or phone number) of an existing user profile before the data reaches the server. This could lead to account takeover through password reset flows.
threats.mitigation: Implement request integrity checks (e.g., HMAC or digital signatures) and ensure the server validates that the Admin has the necessary permissions to update that specific user's attributes.

threats.title: Exposure of Password Hashes
threats.description: An internal attacker or a compromised service intercepts the flow of password hashes from the database to the auth process.
threats.mitigation: Ensure the connection between the App Server and Database is encrypted (Internal TLS).
